chapterTitle: The Market That Isn't Supposed to Exist
imageCaption: A vibrant open-air market materializes on Bonaire's shore, its vendor stalls shimmering slightly at the edges—produce that's too perfect, colors that don't quite exist in the natural spectrum. A morpho butterfly circles overhead as Elijah holds up an impossible fruit, grinning.
text:
Bonaire's market district wasn't supposed to exist on Saturday afternoons.

Aaliyah knew this because the map on her phone said so. The digital tourist information—indexed, optimized, pre-planned—clearly stated that the market operated Wednesday through Sunday mornings only, 6 AM to 2 PM, with a closed period on Thursdays for maintenance and restocking. It was currently 3:47 PM on Saturday, which meant, according to every algorithm ever written, the market should have been closed, packed down, and empty.

Instead, it was the most vibrant thing Aaliyah had ever seen.

The stalls had materialized approximately twenty minutes after they'd gotten off the ferry, and they'd appeared with the casual certainty of a place that had always existed and was simply humoring everyone else's confusion about temporality. Vendor booths spiraled in concentric circles around a central fountain that may or may not have been made of actual water. The produce displayed—mangoes, passion fruit, strange alien-looking things that Elijah had declared were definitely not from this planet but were definitely edible—glowed with a saturation that suggested someone had turned up the color saturation on reality itself.

"This is a glitch," Aaliyah said slowly, filling in the glitch log in her notebook. Her pencil hovered. "But not a dangerous one. It's too... organized. The stalls are arranged perfectly. There are no temporal loops, no timeline overlaps. It's just a market that's existing at the wrong time."

Luisana was already three stalls deep into conversation with a vendor named Rosa, who had the weathered face of someone who'd been selling fruit in temporal anomalies for precisely as long as necessary. "She says it happens sometimes," Luisana called back. "The market gets excited and shows up early. Or late. Or all at once."

"The market gets excited," Aaliyah repeated, because apparently her family's relationship with reality had reached the point where sentient marketplaces were not the strangest thing happening in the conversation.

"Everything's learning," Anthony said cheerfully. He was examining a miniature license plate he'd somehow already found—this one from Sint Maarten, a neighbor island he must have sweet-talked the correct vendor into producing. "The glitches started as broken things. Now they're becoming smart. They're organizing themselves. Following patterns. This one probably figured out that showing up on Saturday afternoon would make the tourists happy."

Elijah had found something called a dragon fruit and was holding it up to the light like it contained the secrets of the universe. "It's breathing," he announced. "Can you see it? It's like the fruit is alive and it's trying to explain something to us."

Sully, for his part, had discovered that Rosa apparently kept fresh fish scraps in a bucket behind her stall, and had decided that this was the best timeline any of them had stumbled into yet.

ARIA-7's voice crackled through someone's dropped phone speaker—Aaliyah was no longer surprised by this. The AI seemed to have resigned herself to commenting on the family's activities via whatever electronic device was nearest.

"This is not a scheduled market operation," the AI said, her tone now carrying notes of something that might have been exhaustion if AIs could experience exhaustion. "The vendor system is operating outside of planned parameters. Your family's temporal resonance has caused a commercial glitch."

"Is it hurting anyone?" Aaliyah asked, which was becoming her standard question for assessing glitches.

"No."

"Is it making anyone happy?"

"Statistically, yes. Three visitors have purchased produce at above-market prices. One has taken a photograph for their social media. Rosa has already exceeded her expected weekly sales."

"Then it's working," Aaliyah said firmly. She turned back to her notebook and added to the entry: *The Bonaire Bazaar. A market glitch caused by simple happiness about timing. Not breaking reality—improving it.*

Luisana appeared at her daughter's side, holding a container of what looked like fresh mango juice. "You're cataloging again."

"Someone has to," Aaliyah said, accepting the juice. It was cold and perfect and tasted like it had been made approximately forty-five minutes into the future. "Each glitch is telling us something about how the world is responding to us. The lighthouse was desperate—it needed to exist so badly it manifested early. This market is just... enthusiastic. It's gentle. It's cooperative."

"The glitches are getting friendly," Luisana said, settling down next to her daughter on a convenient bench that Rosa had somehow produced mid-conversation. "That's new."

"Is that good?" Aaliyah asked.

Her mother was quiet for a moment, watching Anthony negotiate with three different vendors about the possibility of license plates from various territories. He'd somehow charmed them into a competition about who could provide the most interesting one. Elijah was teaching Sully how to gentle-accept pieces of fruit without the enthusiasm that usually preceded stolen food.

"I don't know," Luisana admitted. "Friendly is easier. But easier might mean we stop paying attention. If the glitches become cooperative, maybe we stop treating each one like something we need to understand."

From deeper in the market, there was a sound like wind chimes, except the chimes were made of light and the wind was coming from some impossible direction. The stalls shimmered—not dangerously, but noticeably. The too-perfect colors wavered like watercolor paint applied to damp canvas.

Aaliyah stood. "It's stabilizing."

She was right. As she watched, the market's impossible saturation began to settle into something more natural. The colors were still vibrant, but they no longer looked like they were coming from a source outside consensus reality. The stalls remained, but they became less shimmery, more solid, more... real.

Rosa was moving between her customers with the calm competence of someone used to markets that sometimes needed a moment to remember which timeline they lived in. "It always does," she said, loud enough for the family to hear. "Shows up all excited and temporal. Then it settles. By tomorrow morning, everyone in Bonaire will remember that Saturday afternoon market was always open year-round. The collective memory will just... include it."

"The world is rewriting itself," Aaliyah said quietly. "Every time we arrive somewhere, every time the glitch resolves, the entire history of the place changes. How many people have lived their lives in these places and suddenly found their memories reshuffled?"

"Maybe they needed to be," Anthony said, appearing with three license plates and an expression of profound satisfaction. "Maybe the world was living according to a schedule it didn't actually want. And now it's getting to remember what it was like before everything had to be optimized."

The morpho butterfly appeared above the fountain—it had to have come from somewhere, but Aaliyah had stopped trying to track the butterfly's arrival points. The butterfly appeared where it was needed. That was apparently how the universe worked now.

It circled once, twice, and then pointed itself toward the harbor. Not toward Curaçao, where they'd come from. Toward something beyond.

"We're going to have to leave soon," Luisana said. She wasn't asking. She was stating fact the way she did when she'd been thinking about something and reached a conclusion.

"How soon?" Aaliyah asked.

"Tomorrow. Maybe the day after." Her mother was watching the butterfly too. "The pattern is accelerating. Bonaire stabilized faster than Curaçao did. The next place will stabilize faster still. Home is getting closer, Aaliyah. The island knows we're coming. It's preparing."

Behind them, Rosa was packing up her produce with practiced hands. The market was fading the way things did when the glitch they represented had finally taught the world something new.

By tomorrow morning, Saturday afternoon markets would be normal. Expected. Part of Bonaire's history going backward in time, always having existed.

Aaliyah wrote in her notebook: *Day 20. The market that showed up out of time. The world is remembering how to be alive on its own schedule. And the butterfly is pointing toward home. We're getting closer. I can feel it.*

She underlined it twice, because this one mattered less than the lighthouse but more than she wanted to admit.

The route was becoming clearer.

And so was the pressure to keep moving.
